title: Introduction to fable.gam
text: Just like fable vignette going try forecast number domestic travellers Melbourne, Australia. tsibble::tourism data set, can broken 4 reasons travel: “business”, “holiday”, “visiting friends relatives” “reasons”. variable going try forecast number overnight trips (000s) represented Trips variable. can load dataset visualise time series follows:  Thanks excellent tsibble data structure storing time-series data R know data sampled quarterly. mind, going fit simple time-series GAM using (non)linear trend term seasonal term periodicity 4. made easy fable.gam usage fabletools ‘special’ functions trend season modified fable.gam purposes GAMs. hood, fable.gam parses trend() call modelling smooth function time capture temporal effects season() call smooth function using cyclic cubic basis spline ensure duration time series, start end seasonal term connects (e.g., data measured monthly basis, end final month – December – needs connect continuously start following January). easy fable.gam GAM() function integrated fable interface: can easily pipe rest fable functionality, forecast produce forecasts. automatic forecasts using GAM next 5 years:  quantify model accuracy using accuracy function fable: Another common task extract point forecasts confidence intervals forecast distribution. hilo function distributional package knows automatically handle models fit fable: Hopefully starting highlight power integrating new methods fable framework rather writing bespoke pipelines powerful!
code: tourism_melb <- tourism |>   filter(Region == "Melbourne")  tourism_melb |>   autoplot(Trips) fit <- tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4))) fc <- fit |>   forecast(h = what: Comparison to other common time-series methods
title: Introduction to fable.gam
text: can perform quick sense-check approach commonly-used forecasting methods exponential smoothing. just specify additive trend ETS model let components determined automatically:  maybe want compare ARIMA model:
code: tourism_melb |>   model(     mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4)),     ets = ETS(Trips ~ trend("A"))   ) |>   forecast(h = "5 years") |>   autoplot(tourism_melb) tourism_melb |>   model(     mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4)),     arima = ARIMA(Trips)   ) |>   forecast(h = "5 years") |>   autoplot(tourism_melb)

title: Introduction to fable.gam
text: fable.gam currently equipped handle model families link functions available users familiar fitting GAMs mgcv: Gaussian family link function (e.g., identity) Gamma family log link Poisson family log link Negative binomial family log link Beta family logit (default) probit link current options clean mappings statistical form mgcv distributional package stores vectorised distribution objects easy calculations. test functionality, can simulate weekly count data Poisson distribution small upward trend seasonality inspect raw time series:  can now fit GAM forecast model specify Poisson model family using family() special function designed work fable.gam (note need specify link argument family() using Gaussian model family since log-link used others).  can also fit negative binomial model data comparison:
code: set.seed(123) n_weeks <- 156 start <- as.Date("2022-01-03") dates <- start + (0:(n_weeks - 1)) * 7 t_coef <- seq_len(n_weeks) trend <- 0.004 * t_coef month_no <- lubridate::month(dates) month_fx <- c(-0.35, -0.25, -0.05,  0.10,  0.25,  0.45, 0.50,  0.40,  0.20,  0.00, -0.20, -0.35) seasonal <- month_fx[month_no] log_lambda <- 3.0 + trend + seasonal counts <- rpois(n_weeks, lambda = exp(log_lambda))  # Convert to tsibble and plot to visualise temporal pattern  weekly_counts <- tsibble(Week = yearweek(dates), Counts = counts, index = Week) autoplot(weekly_counts, Counts) weekly_counts |>   model(gam = GAM(Counts ~ trend() + season(12) + season(52) + family(poisson))) |>    forecast(h = "52 weeks") |>   autoplot(weekly_counts) weekly_counts |>   model(gam = GAM(Counts ~ trend() + season(12) + season(52) + family(mgcv::nb()))) |>    forecast(h = "52 weeks") |>   autoplot(weekly_counts)

text: Henderson T (2026). fable.gam: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface fable. R package version 0.3.0, https://hendersontrent.github.io/fable.gam/.

what: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: package provides tidy R interface time-series modelling using generalised additive models (GAMs) using fable. package makes use mgcv package R. particularly common, GAMs shown incredible utility time-series modelling, context palaeoecological data. package aims incorporate broad conceptual approach using structural time-series models (.e., decomposing time series trend, seasonality, error, components modelling additively) within GAM setup incredible fable forecasting framework.

title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: can install development version fable.gam GitHub using following:
code: devtools::install_github("hendersontrent/fable.gam")

title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: Just like fable vignette going try forecast number domestic travellers Melbourne, Australia. tsibble::tourism data set, can broken 4 reasons travel: “business”, “holiday”, “visiting friends relatives” “reasons”. variable going try forecast number overnight trips (000s) represented Trips variable. Thanks excellent tsibble data structure storing time-series data R know data sampled quarterly. mind, going fit simple time-series GAM using (non)linear trend term seasonal term periodicity 4. made easy fable.gam usage fabletools ‘special’ functions trend season modified fable.gam purposes GAMs. hood, fable.gam parses trend() call modelling smooth function time capture temporal effects season() call smooth function using cyclic cubic basis spline ensure duration time series, start end seasonal term connects (e.g., data measured monthly basis, end final month – December – needs connect continuously start following January). easy fable.gam GAM() function integrated fable interface: can easily pipe rest fable functionality, forecast produce forecasts. automatic forecasts using GAM next 5 years:  quantify model accuracy using accuracy function fable: Another common task extract point forecasts confidence intervals forecast distribution. hilo function distributional package knows automatically handle models fit fable: Hopefully starting highlight power integrating new methods fable framework rather writing bespoke pipelines powerful!
code: library(tsibble) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(fable) library(fable.gam) library(tsibbledata) tourism_melb <- tsibble::tourism |>   filter(Region == "Melbourne")  fit <- tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4))) fc <- fit |>   forecast(h = "5 years")  fc |>   autoplot(tourism_melb) Warning: `autoplot.fbl_ts()` was deprecated in fabletools 0.6.0. ℹ Please use `ggtime::autoplot.fbl_ts()` instead. ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use   `library(ggtime)` instead. This warning is displayed once per session. Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was generated. fit |>   accuracy() |>   arrange(MASE) # A tibble: 4 × 13   Region    State  Purpose .model .type        ME  RMSE   MAE    MPE  MAPE  MASE   <chr>     <chr>  <chr>   <chr>  <chr>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> 1 Melbourne Victo… Busine… mygam  Trai…  1.62e-14  52.0  39.7 -1.44   8.88 0.640 2 Melbourne Victo… Visiti… mygam  Trai…  9.20e-14  52.1  41.4 -0.751  6.76 0.660 3 Melbourne Victo… Holiday mygam  Trai…  1.37e-13  50.1  38.9 -0.990  7.79 0.706 4 Melbourne Victo… Other   mygam  Trai… -6.66e-15  19.1  15.8 -4.64  18.1  0.710 # ℹ 2 more variables: RMSSE <dbl>, ACF1 <dbl> fc |>   hilo(level = c(80, 95)) # A tsibble: 80 x 9 [1Q] # Key:       Region, State, Purpose, .model [4]    Region    State    Purpose  .model Quarter    <chr>     <chr>    <chr>    <chr>    <qtr>  1 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2018 Q1  2 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2018 Q2  3 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2018 Q3  4 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2018 Q4  5 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2019 Q1  6 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2019 Q2  7 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2019 Q3  8 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2019 Q4  9 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2020 Q1 10 Melbourne Victoria Business mygam  2020 Q2 # ℹ 70 more rows # ℹ 4 more variables: Trips <dist>, .mean <dbl>, `80%` <hilo>, `95%` <hilo>

what: Comparison to other common time-series methods
title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: can perform quick sense-check approach commonly-used forecasting methods exponential smoothing. just specify additive trend ETS model let components determined automatically. also log-transform response variable show fable.gam handles transformations automatically well, just like models fable.
code: tourism_melb |>   model(     mygam = GAM(log(Trips) ~ trend() + season(4)),     ets = ETS(log(Trips) ~ trend("A"))   ) |>   forecast(h = "5 years") |>   autoplot(tourism_melb)

what: Controlling the smooth function of the time trend
title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: addition trend() season() special functions, fable.gam also includes special trend2() lets users control smooth term fit temporal trend. trend2() takes arguments k (dimension basis used represent smooth term; .e., number knots) bs (two letter character string indicating smoothing basis use). example specify basis 5 Gaussian process smooth:
code: tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend2(k = 5, bs = "gp") + season(4))) |>   forecast(h = "5 years") |>   autoplot(tourism_melb)

title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: suite fable functions interpreting trained models also available GAM models fit using fable.gam. glance() one example:
code: tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend2(k = 5, bs = "gp") + season(4))) |>   glance() # A tibble: 4 × 13   Region State Purpose .model r_squared deviance    df log_lik   AIC   BIC   edf   <chr>  <chr> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> 1 Melbo… Vict… Busine… mygam      0.569  235947.  74.2   -433.  880.  896.  5.79 2 Melbo… Vict… Holiday mygam      0.752  197173.  74.9   -426.  864.  878.  5.06 3 Melbo… Vict… Other   mygam      0.641   31195.  75.9   -352.  714.  726.  4.06 4 Melbo… Vict… Visiti… mygam      0.640  231727.  73.4   -432.  880.  898.  6.61 # ℹ 2 more variables: GCV <dbl>, scale <dbl>

title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: fable.gam also permits ability specify autocorrelated errors capture residual serial correlation, just like can using gamm instead gam mgcv. another example fpp3 seasonal, autocorrelated data small trend (note addition fable.gam special errors(ar = 2) captures autocorrelation lag 2 identified fpp3).
code: h02 <- tsibbledata::PBS |>   filter(ATC2 == "H02") |>   summarise(Cost = sum(Cost) / 1e6)  h02 |>   model(     mygam = GAM(log(Cost) ~ trend() + season(12) + errors(ar = 2)),     arima = ARIMA(log(Cost) ~ 0 + pdq(3, 0, 1) + PDQ(0, 1, 2))     ) |>   forecast() |>   autoplot(h02) +   labs(y = " $AU (millions)",        title = "Corticosteroid drug scripts (H02) sales") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Generalised Additive Modelling Interface for fable
text: fable.gam currently equipped handle model families link functions available users familiar fitting GAMs mgcv: Gaussian family link function (e.g., identity) Gamma family log link Poisson family log link Negative binomial family log link current options clean mappings statistical form mgcv distributional package stores vectorised distribution objects easy calculations. test functionality, can simulate weekly count data Poisson distribution small upward trend seasonality inspect raw time series:  can now fit GAM forecast model specify Poisson model family using family() special function designed work fable.gam (note need specify link argument family() using Gaussian model family since log-link used others).  can also fit negative binomial model data comparison:
code: set.seed(123) n_weeks <- 156 start <- as.Date("2022-01-03") dates <- start + (0:(n_weeks - 1)) * 7 t_coef <- seq_len(n_weeks) trend <- 0.004 * t_coef month_no <- lubridate::month(dates) month_fx <- c(-0.35, -0.25, -0.05,  0.10,  0.25,  0.45, 0.50,  0.40,  0.20,  0.00, -0.20, -0.35) seasonal <- month_fx[month_no] log_lambda <- 3.0 + trend + seasonal counts <- rpois(n_weeks, lambda = exp(log_lambda))  # Convert to tsibble and plot to visualise temporal pattern  weekly_counts <- tsibble(Week = yearweek(dates), Counts = counts, index = Week) autoplot(weekly_counts, Counts) weekly_counts |>   model(gam = GAM(Counts ~ trend() + season(12) + season(52) + family(poisson))) |>    forecast(h = "52 weeks") |>   autoplot(weekly_counts) weekly_counts |>   model(gam = GAM(Counts ~ trend() + season(12) + season(52) + family(mgcv::nb()))) |>    forecast(h = "52 weeks") |>   autoplot(weekly_counts)

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: Prepares generalised additive model specification use within `fable` package.

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
code: GAM(formula, ...)

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: formula symbolic description model fitted class `formula` ... arguments passing e.g. gam.fit3 (mustart).

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: `trend` special used specify trend component
code: trend()

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: `trend2` special used specify nonlinear trend component modelled via smooth functions
code: trend2()

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: `season` special used specify seasonal component. special can used multiple times different seasonalities. **NOTE: inputs controlling seasonal `period` refer number time points seasonal period.**
code: season(period = NULL)

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: Exogenous regressors can included via `xreg` special. smooth TRUE, numeric variables automatically wrapped s() smooth function estimation k Basis dimension passed s(). Defaults -1 (whereby mgcv automatically chooses k). used smooth = TRUE bs Spline basis type passed s(). Defaults "tp" (thin plate). used smooth = TRUE
code: xreg(..., smooth = TRUE, k = -1, bs = "tp")

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: `errors` special adds autoregressive correlation structure model residuals. specified, model fit using `gamm()` instead `gam()`. ar Order autoregressive error process. Must positive integer. Defaults 1
code: errors(ar = 1)

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: `family` special sets response family link function, passed directly mgcv::gam(). Defaults gaussian(link = "identity") family Family function (e.g. Gamma) family function link function (e.g. Gamma(link = "log")) link Link function name character string (e.g. "log")
code: family(family = gaussian, link = NULL)

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
text: Trent Henderson

title: Generalised additive modelling — GAM
code: # \donttest{ library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(tsibble) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘tsibble’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, union  tourism_melb <- tsibble::tourism |>   filter(Region == "Melbourne") |>   filter(Purpose == "Business") |>   dplyr::select(c(Quarter, Region, Trips)) |>   as_tsibble(key = Region, index = Quarter)  fit <- tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4))) |>   forecast(h = "5 years") # }

title: Produce forecasts from the GAM — forecast.fbl_gam
code: # \donttest{ library(dplyr) library(tsibble)  tourism_melb <- tsibble::tourism |>   filter(Region == "Melbourne") |>   filter(Purpose == "Business") |>   dplyr::select(c(Quarter, Region, Trips)) |>   as_tsibble(key = Region, index = Quarter)  fit <- tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4)))  # Analytic intervals  forecast(fit, h = "5 years") #> # A fable: 20 x 5 [1Q] #> # Key:     Region, .model [1] #>    Region    .model Quarter #>    <chr>     <chr>    <qtr> #>  1 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q1 #>  2 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q2 #>  3 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q3 #>  4 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q4 #>  5 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q1 #>  6 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q2 #>  7 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q3 #>  8 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q4 #>  9 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q1 #> 10 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q2 #> 11 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q3 #> 12 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q4 #> 13 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q1 #> 14 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q2 #> 15 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q3 #> 16 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q4 #> 17 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q1 #> 18 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q2 #> 19 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q3 #> 20 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q4 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: Trips <dist>, .mean <dbl>  # Bootstrap intervals  forecast(fit, h = "5 years", bootstrap = TRUE) #> # A fable: 20 x 5 [1Q] #> # Key:     Region, .model [1] #>    Region    .model Quarter        Trips .mean #>    <chr>     <chr>    <qtr>       <dist> <dbl> #>  1 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q1 sample[5000]  670. #>  2 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q2 sample[5000]  729. #>  3 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q3 sample[5000]  760. #>  4 Melbourne mygam  2018 Q4 sample[5000]  716. #>  5 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q1 sample[5000]  732. #>  6 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q2 sample[5000]  788. #>  7 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q3 sample[5000]  821. #>  8 Melbourne mygam  2019 Q4 sample[5000]  776. #>  9 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q1 sample[5000]  790. #> 10 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q2 sample[5000]  849. #> 11 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q3 sample[5000]  878. #> 12 Melbourne mygam  2020 Q4 sample[5000]  837. #> 13 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q1 sample[5000]  853. #> 14 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q2 sample[5000]  910. #> 15 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q3 sample[5000]  940. #> 16 Melbourne mygam  2021 Q4 sample[5000]  898. #> 17 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q1 sample[5000]  912. #> 18 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q2 sample[5000]  970. #> 19 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q3 sample[5000] 1001. #> 20 Melbourne mygam  2022 Q4 sample[5000]  958. # text: `tibble` summarising model fit.

title: Glance a GAM — glance.fbl_gam
code: # \donttest{ library(dplyr) library(tsibble)  tourism_melb <- tsibble::tourism |>   filter(Region == "Melbourne") |>   filter(Purpose == "Business") |>   dplyr::select(c(Quarter, Region, Trips)) |>   as_tsibble(key = Region, index = Quarter)  fit <- tourism_melb |>   model(mygam = GAM(Trips ~ trend() + season(4))) |>   glance() # }
